q: For a fraud detection system with a 0.1% positive rate, which offline metric most clearly exposes how often the model confuses the rare positive class with negatives?
options: AUC-PR, since it evaluates precision-recall tradeoffs and collapses when false positives swamp the rare true positives
AUC-ROC, since it aggregates true-positive and false-positive rates across all thresholds and is unaffected by class ratios
Accuracy, since overall prediction counts stay interpretable and stable when the majority class dominates the distribution
Log Loss, since it penalizes miscalibrated probability outputs and applies identically regardless of positive-class frequency
explanation: AUC-ROC can remain high even when a model generates massive false positives because the huge negative count normalizes the FPR axis. AUC-PR is far more sensitive: each false positive visibly depresses precision and degrades the curve.

q: The Population Stability Index (PSI) is used in production ML monitoring to:
options: Estimate the minimum user count required before launching a statistically valid A/B test on a new model version
Quantify how much a feature's statistical distribution has shifted between the training snapshot and current live traffic
Measure what fraction of prediction requests exceed the configured p99 latency threshold in the serving infrastructure
Track how model accuracy degrades per week as the training dataset ages beyond the configured recency window
explanation: PSI measures distributional shift per feature between two time periods. A PSI > 0.2 indicates significant drift and typically triggers a retraining alert. It is distinct from latency monitoring or experiment sizing.

q: Google's ScaNN (Scalable Nearest Neighbors) library achieves its best accuracy-speed tradeoff by specifically optimizing for which vector similarity operation?
options: Euclidean (L2) nearest-neighbor search over image feature vectors using product quantization partitioned across GPUs
Cosine similarity search over high-dimensional sparse text embeddings generated by classical term-frequency pipelines
Maximum inner product search (MIPS) with anisotropic vector quantization calibrated to the live query distribution
Hamming distance search over compact binary hash codes deployed in near-duplicate video and audio deduplication pipelines
explanation: ScaNN uses anisotropic quantization tuned for MIPS, the operation when embeddings interact via dot product. This gives ~2x speed over HNSW at equal recall on Google retrieval workloads and powers YouTube, Search, and Ads candidate generation.

q: A key operational disadvantage of HNSW for approximate nearest-neighbor search at billion-scale, compared to FAISS IVF-PQ, is:
options: HNSW cannot achieve recall above 85% at any configuration, limiting its applicability to high-accuracy retrieval workloads
HNSW requires scanning the entire index in a sequential linear sweep for each new query, making its latency scale directly with the total corpus size and ruling out billion-scale deployments
HNSW's graph traversal is significantly slower than FAISS flat-index brute-force at identical recall operating points
HNSW's multi-layer graph stores many inter-node pointers, consuming substantially more memory per embedding than product-quantized indexes
explanation: HNSW achieves >95% recall and supports dynamic inserts, but the graph stores many neighbor pointers per node. FAISS IVF-PQ compresses embeddings with product quantization, shrinking index size at a recall cost. The memory tradeoff is HNSW's primary weakness.

q: In the Google model compression playbook, knowledge distillation produces a deployable model by:
options: Training a small student on the teacher's soft output distribution, achieving 10-100x fewer parameters at roughly 1-5% accuracy loss
Removing layers whose average gradient magnitude falls below a threshold during a fine-tuning pass on the held-out validation set
Sharing the teacher's embedding weights directly with the student so that pre-trained representations are inherited without retraining
Replacing the teacher's softmax outputs with hard one-hot targets to reduce the student's memory footprint during inference
explanation: Distillation trains the student to match the teacher's probability distribution over all classes (soft labels), not just the argmax. Soft labels encode dark knowledge about inter-class similarities, enabling smaller models to generalize better than hard-label training.

q: Log Loss (binary cross-entropy) is preferred over AUC-ROC as the ads click prediction training objective because:
options: Log Loss produces faster stochastic gradient descent convergence on sparse categorical ad features than ranking surrogates
Log Loss penalizes miscalibrated probability estimates — critical when P(click) multiplies directly into the auction bid price
Log Loss is inherently less sensitive to extreme class imbalance in ad datasets where positive rates fall below 1% overall
Log Loss optimizes the ranking order of predicted click scores rather than their absolute values, aligning with ad selection goals
explanation: Ad rank = bid × P(click) × quality_score. If P(click) is systematically over- or under-estimated, the auction charges advertisers incorrectly, destroying trust and revenue. Log Loss minimizes calibration error; AUC-ROC does not.

q: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) is the most appropriate offline ranking metric when:
options: Measuring how many relevant items appear at any position across all K slots in a deep ranked result list
Comparing rankers on an evaluation set where each query has five or more labeled relevant documents at various depths
The user's primary goal is finding one single best answer quickly, as in a navigational or direct-answer query
Estimating a calibrated probability that the top-ranked item will actually receive a click from the querying user
explanation: MRR = average of 1/rank_of_first_relevant. It rewards placing the first relevant item as high as possible, regardless of further results. It's ideal for single-answer tasks like entity lookups but misses whether a comprehensive list was returned.

q: The gap period intentionally inserted between the training window and the validation window in a time-based data split is designed to:
options: Prevent gradient updates from one distributed training worker from contaminating another worker's validation data shard
Allow newly uploaded items to accumulate enough interactions before they appear in the validation evaluation set
Simulate the production model refresh cadence so offline metric timing aligns with the typical live experiment schedule
Prevent label leakage when the target (e.g., purchase within 7 days) requires future events that haven't occurred at the training cutoff
explanation: Without a gap, validation labels overlap with training data in time, allowing future events to inform training examples. A gap equal to the label delay window ensures the evaluation truly simulates prospective prediction.

q: The primary limitation of backtesting a new ranking model on historical impression logs is:
options: Logged data only contains items the prior production model chose to show, so items the new model would surface remain unobserved
Replaying historical click logs reverses temporal ordering, causing future click patterns to leak into the evaluation period
Historical queries are shorter on average than current queries, causing the evaluation to underestimate accuracy on long-tail inputs
Backtesting metrics require synchronized wall-clock timestamps across logging shards, which is hard to guarantee at planet scale
explanation: Selection bias in logs makes backtesting incomplete: the corpus is filtered by what the baseline model ranked highly. Items the challenger would retrieve but the baseline never showed have no recorded user actions and are invisible to offline evaluation.

q: Deep & Cross Network v2 (DCN-v2) improves on Wide & Deep for click-through rate prediction primarily because:
options: DCN-v2 uses per-feature-family embedding tables that reduce hash collision compared to Wide & Deep's single shared table
DCN-v2's explicit cross network layers learn bounded-degree feature interactions automatically, removing manual cross-feature engineering
DCN-v2 adds residual skip connections at every layer depth, enabling stable training of networks with hundreds of stacked layers
DCN-v2 replaces the deep DNN component with a transformer encoder to model longer-range interaction patterns across all input features
explanation: Wide & Deep requires engineers to manually define which features to cross in the Wide component. DCN-v2's Cross Network learns feature interaction weights at each layer via a structured matrix, eliminating hand-crafted cross-product features.

q: Focal loss addresses class imbalance differently from class-weight reweighting by:
options: Scaling each batch by the class imbalance ratio before each gradient step, equivalent to dynamically upsampling the minority class
Adding a logarithmic penalty to very high-confidence correct predictions to reduce the overconfident calibration of the majority class
Down-weighting the contribution of easy, already-correct majority examples so gradients are dominated by hard or uncertain cases
Appending a contrastive margin term between positive and negative class logits in the style of a pairwise hinge-loss function
explanation: Focal loss multiplies standard cross-entropy by (1 - p_t)^gamma. Easy examples with high p_t get a near-zero weight. Hard or misclassified examples drive training. This differs from class-weight reweighting, which scales by class frequency uniformly.

q: Inverse propensity scoring (IPS) is applied to recommendation training data to correct for the fact that:
options: Rare items with few impressions generate synthetic positive examples that inflate their estimated engagement rates
Explicit user ratings cluster at extremes (1 star or 5 stars), biasing the label distribution away from mid-range quality signals
Session-level features computed at training time use future information unavailable in the real-time serving pipeline
Items ranked lower by the current system receive proportionally fewer clicks than equally relevant items shown at the top
explanation: IPS weights each training example by 1/P(item was shown), giving higher weight to clicks on rarely-displayed items. A click on an item shown 1% of the time is a stronger relevance signal than a click on an item shown 90% of the time.

q: Pipeline parallelism in large-scale distributed model training is the right strategy specifically when:
options: The model is too large to fit on a single device, so different micro-batches flow through model stages on different devices simultaneously
Training data exceeds the storage of a single machine and must be partitioned across many workers with AllReduce gradient aggregation
Gradient communication latency becomes the bottleneck and overlapping compute with network transfers requires micro-batch pipelining
Mixed-precision arithmetic is unavailable, forcing full float32 operations to be spread across multiple chips to fit in device memory
explanation: Pipeline parallelism (GPipe, PaLM) assigns different model stages to different devices. Micro-batches flow through the pipeline continuously to maintain device utilization. Data parallelism and tensor parallelism are distinct strategies for different bottlenecks.

q: A canary release of a new ML model typically starts by routing what initial fraction of traffic to the new model?
options: 0.01% — limited to internal employees only, verifying serving correctness before any external user encounters the new model
1-5% — a small real-user slice to monitor genuine business metric impact with controlled blast radius before gradual expansion
25% — a statistically powered A/B bucket providing significance on key metrics within a standard 48-hour measurement window
50% — a full two-arm split that maximizes statistical power and minimizes the calendar time needed to reach significance
explanation: A 1-5% canary gives enough real traffic to detect latency regressions, error rate spikes, and business metric changes while limiting the number of users affected if the model has unexpected behavior or serving bugs.

q: Which scenario correctly describes a harmful positive feedback loop in a production recommendation system?
options: A fraud model flags transactions for human review, investigators confirm true labels, and the confirmed labels retrain a more precise model
Users provide explicit thumbs-up ratings, the ratings are aggregated into training labels, and the new model surfaces more highly rated content
A recommender shows popular items, popular items get more clicks, training reinforces their ranking, and niche content stops receiving impressions
An A/B test confirms a model improvement, triggering a gradual rollout that steadily replaces the old model across the entire user base
explanation: Popularity bias creates a self-reinforcing cycle: popular items accumulate clicks faster, training amplifies their scores, they dominate recommendations even more, and the long tail receives no exposure or feedback signal.

q: Thompson sampling manages the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in recommendation candidate generation by:
options: Selecting items at random from a uniform distribution over the entire catalog at a fixed epsilon exploration rate per request
Greedily picking the item with the highest predicted reward, then injecting Gaussian noise to probabilistically diversify selections
Computing an upper confidence bound (UCB) on each item's reward estimate and selecting the item with the highest UCB value
Sampling from a maintained probability distribution over each item's expected reward, naturally balancing exploration and exploitation
explanation: Thompson sampling draws a reward sample from each item's posterior distribution and picks the argmax. Items with wider posteriors (fewer observations) get sampled to a high value more often, automatically driving exploration of uncertain items.

q: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) penalizes prediction errors more heavily than Mean Absolute Error (MAE) because:
options: RMSE squares each error before averaging, so one large error contributes quadratically more than many small errors of equal total magnitude
RMSE measures relative deviations scaled by the true label, causing percentage errors at large prediction values to dominate the metric
RMSE assigns adaptive sample weights based on prediction difficulty, amplifying harder examples' influence on the final loss value
RMSE reports the maximum observed error per mini-batch rather than the mean, making it sensitive to any individual outlier prediction
explanation: RMSE = sqrt(mean(e^2)). Squaring converts a single error of magnitude 10 into a contribution of 100, versus ten errors of 1 contributing only 10 total. This makes RMSE appropriate when large prediction errors are disproportionately costly.

q: To reduce label noise caused by users immediately bouncing back after clicking a search result, the most direct and targeted intervention is:
options: Downsampling non-clicked impressions to achieve a balanced 1:1 positive-to-negative ratio before each training epoch
Requiring a minimum post-click dwell time (e.g., >N seconds) before recording any interaction as a positive training signal
Applying label smoothing that uniformly lowers all positive label values from 1.0 to a fixed soft target such as 0.7
Using separate loss weights for in-session versus cross-session clicks to explicitly account for recency differences in signal quality
explanation: Requiring dwell time > N seconds filters out curiosity clicks and back-button bounces, retaining only interactions where content was actually consumed. This directly improves label precision without altering the class distribution.

q: For a newly uploaded video with no watch history, which strategy best initializes its embedding in the two-tower retrieval model?
options: Exclude new videos from candidate generation entirely until they accumulate 1,000 interactions from randomly selected users
Assign the video the centroid embedding of its channel, computed as the average over all prior video embeddings from that creator
Derive an initial embedding from content features (title, description, category), then seed with exploratory traffic to gather interaction signals
Borrow the most-recently-published video's embedding from the same category as a warm-start prior until interactions arrive
explanation: The item tower can embed new videos from their content alone, placing them in the correct region of the embedding space from day zero. Exploratory traffic then converts content-based placement into interaction-based refinement rapidly.

q: Google's practice of permanently keeping 1-5% of users on the old model as a long-term holdout is primarily designed to measure:
options: Training-serving skew by comparing feature distribution statistics between holdout users and experiment-arm users over several weeks
Assignment bias in A/B experiments by confirming that holdout and treatment user cohorts have identical covariate distributions
Infrastructure scaling regressions that only appear when the new model handles a supermajority of production traffic simultaneously
Long-term behavioral effects — habituation, content fatigue, or filter bubbles — that only manifest weeks to months after deployment
explanation: Short A/B tests capture novelty effects and immediate engagement. Long-term holdouts reveal whether quality improvements persist after users habituate or whether side effects like filter bubbles and session fatigue accumulate over time.

q: At 100,000 queries per second, engineers focus on p99 latency rather than p50 because:
options: p99 = 200ms means 1,000 users experience a slow response every second, and multi-shard fan-out compounds the tail multiplicatively
The median user's request naturally falls in the p99 tail once the load distribution is weighted by request complexity and feature count
SLA contracts for cloud inference are billed at the 99th percentile, making it the compliance target with direct and measurable cost impact
p99 latency is driven by a small, identifiable subset of outlier requests, making it easier to diagnose and optimize than the median
explanation: 1,000 slow responses per second matters at scale. Fan-out amplifies the tail further: if a request fans out to 10 shards each with p99=100ms, the probability of at least one shard hitting its tail is ~65%, making the effective system p99 much worse.

q: BLEU and ROUGE are considered insufficient as the primary evaluation metrics for modern open-ended language generation because:
options: They require professional human annotation to compute, making them prohibitively slow and expensive for automated evaluation pipelines
They measure n-gram overlap with a reference answer, which correlates poorly with human quality judgments when many valid responses exist
They were originally designed for sentence-level tasks and cannot be reliably applied to paragraph-length LLM-generated responses
They reward high-frequency token usage, causing models optimized by these metrics to overuse common phrases at the expense of accuracy
explanation: For open-ended generation, diverse valid responses differ substantially from any single reference. A paraphrase that is semantically equivalent to the reference but lexically distinct scores near zero, making n-gram overlap a misleading quality proxy.

q: Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) is applied in the re-ranking stage of a recommendation system to:
options: Boost candidate items whose predicted engagement score ranks highest among those not yet placed in the final result list
Penalize candidate items with high textual similarity to the user's query, increasing keyword diversity in the final result set
Iteratively select items that balance high predicted relevance with low similarity to already-selected items, promoting diversity
Down-weight candidates the user interacted with in earlier sessions to ensure the feed surfaces exclusively fresh, unseen content
explanation: MMR selects the next item maximizing lambda × relevance - (1-lambda) × similarity_to_selected. This prevents clustering redundant items at the top while still prioritizing relevance, directly countering popularity-driven filter bubbles.

q: In a multi-task ranking model with separate prediction heads for click rate, watch time, and satisfaction, the shared lower layers primarily serve to:
options: Merge separate per-task embedding tables into a single compressed layer that reduces GPU memory and avoids hash collisions
Provide gradient highways that prevent vanishing gradients from blocking backpropagation through the task-specific upper prediction heads
Enforce hard constraints preventing one task head from sacrificing its objective metric to improve another conflicting objective
Learn universal user and item representations useful across all tasks, while upper task-specific layers specialize for each prediction head
explanation: Shared lower layers develop rich generic representations capturing user preferences and item semantics useful for every task. Task-specific upper layers then specialize without each having to learn basic embeddings from scratch, improving data efficiency.

q: In a production RAG pipeline, reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) is applied after retrieval to:
options: Merge ranked lists from dense embedding retrieval and sparse BM25 into a unified list without requiring a learned score combiner
Rerank the top-50 retrieved passages by jointly attending to the full query and passage text via a cross-encoder architecture
Filter retrieved passages whose cosine similarity to the query embedding falls below a configured minimum relevance cutoff
Partition retrieved passages into semantic clusters and select the top-scoring representative passage from each distinct cluster
explanation: RRF computes sum(1/(k+rank_i)) across retrieval systems and re-sorts by this combined score. It merges heterogeneous ranked lists without requiring calibrated scores and consistently outperforms simple score averaging in hybrid retrieval setups.

q: Dense embedding-based retrieval is preferred over a BM25 inverted index specifically for queries that require:
options: Exact keyword matching against high-frequency terms where the index contains pre-computed term frequencies from a large crawl
Semantic understanding — matching paraphrases, near-synonyms, or cross-language queries — where exact term overlap is insufficient
Sub-millisecond retrieval latency on massive indexes where approximate-neighbor errors introduce unacceptable relevance variance
Navigational lookup where the user targets a specific known URL and term-based matching provides precise, deterministic results
explanation: Dense embeddings capture meaning across surface forms, retrieving semantically equivalent but lexically different documents. BM25 excels for exact-term matching. Production systems typically run both in parallel and merge via reciprocal rank fusion.

q: Platt scaling is applied as a post-processing step on an ads click prediction model specifically to:
options: Increase the model's discriminative power by transforming raw logit outputs into better-separated score ranges for the auction ranking
Reduce overfitting in the final sigmoid layer by learning a regularized scale factor that dampens extreme logit magnitudes
Correct systematic over- or under-estimation of click probabilities by fitting a sigmoid on a held-out calibration dataset
Map raw model outputs to bid-compatible monetary units so P(click) and cost-per-click values share a common interpretable scale
explanation: Platt scaling learns a and b in sigma(a*score + b) on held-out data to align predicted probabilities with observed frequencies. A model with high AUC (good ranking) can still have poor calibration (wrong absolute probabilities), and Platt fixes the latter.

q: Building a transaction graph where nodes are accounts and edges represent shared devices, IPs, or beneficiaries is used in fraud detection to:
options: Compute velocity features by counting the number of transactions a single account performs within configurable rolling time windows
Identify account takeover fraud by detecting login sessions originating from IP ranges outside a user's established location history
Surface merchant-side collusion patterns by linking fraudulent chargebacks to shared terminal identifiers across multiple merchant IDs
Uncover fraud rings where multiple synthetic or mule accounts share infrastructure to coordinate organized identity-theft schemes
explanation: Individual-account signals miss coordinated fraud. Graph structures expose clusters of accounts sharing a device fingerprint, IP subnet, or beneficiary account — the defining characteristic of synthetic identity rings and money mule networks.

q: In a multi-stage search ranking pipeline, a BERT cross-encoder is applied only in Phase 2 on the top ~100 candidates rather than on all retrieved documents because:
options: Cross-encoders jointly encode query and document, making inference O(N) per query — affordable for 100 candidates but infeasible for 1,000+
BERT embeddings are less well-calibrated for relevance scoring than the gradient-boosted tree features used in Phase-1 BM25 scoring
Cross-encoder models require a separately fine-tuned document quality classifier that must run before any cross-attention computation starts
Phase 2 uses CPU-based hardware while Phase 1 runs on GPUs, and BERT cross-encoders achieve their highest efficiency on CPUs only
explanation: A bi-encoder pre-indexes items offline; a cross-encoder must process every (query, document) pair at query time, so cost is linear in candidate count. Applying it to all 1,000 retrieved documents would exceed the latency budget for a <200ms system.

q: Which task is NOT part of query understanding that runs before document retrieval in a search ranking pipeline?
options: Spelling correction and synonym expansion to normalize natural-language variations in the raw user-typed query string
Encoding the query and each retrieved document jointly through a cross-encoder to produce a ranked relevance score per document
Named-entity recognition to identify persons, places, or products mentioned explicitly in the search query text
Intent classification to distinguish navigational, informational, and transactional query types before retrieval begins
explanation: Query understanding covers linguistic preprocessing before retrieval: spell correction, expansion, entity tagging, and intent detection. Cross-encoder joint scoring happens in Phase-2 ranking after a short candidate list has already been retrieved.

q: Velocity features — counts of transactions in the last 1, 5, and 60 minutes — are often the strongest predictors in fraud detection because:
options: They are derived from payment-network external data sources that cannot be manipulated by a fraudulent account holder
Rolling-window aggregations capture richer behavioral context than any single transaction record's standalone feature values
A sudden spike in transaction frequency within a short time window is a strong fraud signal even when individual transactions appear normal
Velocity computations are performed server-side in streaming pipelines, making them immune to manipulation by user-provided input data
explanation: Fraudsters typically exhaust stolen card credentials rapidly. Ten transactions in five minutes from geographically dispersed merchants is anomalous regardless of each individual transaction's face validity, making velocity the canonical fraud signal.

q: In Google's ad auction, what pricing mechanism determines the winning advertiser's actual cost per click?
options: First-price auction: the winner pays their exact bid multiplied by the predicted click probability for that specific impression
Uniform clearing price: all competing advertisers pay the same market-clearing price based on average slot historical CPCs
Cost-per-thousand (CPM) pricing: the winner is charged per thousand impressions regardless of how many actual clicks occur
Second-price (generalized second-price or VCG) auction: the winner pays based on the next-highest competing bid
explanation: Second-price auctions incentivize truthful bidding because bidding more than your value never increases profit and bidding less risks losing a valuable slot. The winner pays only what is necessary to beat the next-highest offer, not their own maximum.

q: Counterfactual explanations in ML are most valuable for which specific stakeholder scenario?
options: Explaining to a loan applicant what changes to their profile would convert a denial decision into an approval
Auditing a model globally to detect which demographic proxies it relies on across all predictions in a training corpus
Providing a model card to regulators that documents training data characteristics, known failure modes, and performance bounds
Debugging a geographic performance gap by comparing feature distributions between well-performing and underperforming regions
explanation: Counterfactuals answer 'what would need to change?' — directly actionable for individuals subject to automated decisions. Global feature importance, model cards, and geographic debugging serve different stakeholders and address different questions.

q: The 'equal opportunity' fairness constraint applied to a content moderation classifier specifically requires that:
options: The total fraction of content flagged as policy-violating must be statistically equal across all demographic groups of creators
True positive rates (recall for genuine violations) must be equal across groups, catching violations at the same rate across all creators
The predicted violation probability distribution must be identically shaped across every user segment viewing the flagged content
Human review hours and moderation costs must be allocated proportionally to each region's total content upload volume
explanation: Equal opportunity equates TPR: P(predicted_violating | truly_violating, group=A) = P(predicted_violating | truly_violating, group=B). Demographic parity equalizes overall positive prediction rates — a strictly different and often incompatible constraint.

q: Interpreting attention weights as feature attributions — concluding that attending to a token means it caused the prediction — is problematic because:
options: Attention weights are computed only during training and require special logging hooks to be accessible at production inference time
Attention matrices are too high-dimensional for non-technical stakeholders to interpret or summarize in a practically meaningful way
A model can attend heavily to a token due to spurious correlation without that token causally influencing the prediction outcome
Attention weights are architecture-specific and cannot be compared meaningfully across BERT variants with different layer configurations
explanation: Attention measures where the model looked, not why. Models can correlate attendance with uninformative tokens. Methods like SHAP and integrated gradients measure causal attribution rigorously; raw attention weights do not.

q: Dynamic request batching at the model serving layer improves GPU throughput because:
options: Pre-fetching the next request while serving the current one prevents pipeline stalls and reduces effective per-request latency overhead
Deduplicating feature values shared across requests in a time window reduces data transfer overhead to the model server
Retaining model weights in GPU shared memory for longer between requests reduces the overhead of repeated parameter loading
Grouping arriving requests into one matrix operation lets the GPU process many inputs in parallel in a single forward pass
explanation: GPUs excel at large matrix-matrix operations. Batching N requests turns N sequential matrix-vector products into one matrix-matrix product, achieving near-peak tensor core utilization and dramatically improving throughput at a small latency cost per request.

q: A multi-modal content moderation system fuses signals across video frames, audio transcripts, and text metadata because:
options: Bad actors embed violations across modalities — text in image frames, harmful audio under clean video — so each modality alone misses cross-modal evasions
Regulatory standards require equal weighting for each input modality to prevent modality-based enforcement bias claims by content creators
Audio classification latency is substantially higher than video or text, requiring fusion to synchronize outputs before a decision is issued
Classifiers for different modalities output probabilities on incompatible numerical scales that must be re-normalized before threshold application
explanation: Adversarial creators exploit single-modality blind spots. Cross-modal consistency checks catch cases where one modality appears clean while another signals a violation — for example, innocent video with a harmful audio overlay.

q: A language model with perplexity 32 on a held-out corpus means the model is on average:
options: Incorrectly predicting 32% of all tokens in the evaluation set according to a greedy decoding evaluation protocol
As uncertain at each prediction step as if it were choosing uniformly among 32 equally probable next-token candidates
Requiring approximately 32 beam-search steps before the correct token's cumulative probability rises above the greedy threshold
Producing a per-token cross-entropy of log_2(32) = 5 bits when computed strictly in base-2 entropy measurement units
explanation: Perplexity = exp(average NLL). Perplexity 32 means the model's effective branching factor is 32 choices per step. Lower perplexity means the model is more confident and accurate; a perfect bigram model over a 2-word corpus has perplexity 2.

q: Query rewriting is performed as the first step in a RAG pipeline specifically to:
options: Expand the user query with synonyms and topically related terms to improve recall from the sparse BM25 retrieval index
Screen whether the query contains safety-sensitive content before any retrieval calls are issued to the knowledge base
Transform a conversational message into a standalone search query by resolving coreferences from prior chat turns
Compress the user query to fit within the maximum token budget of the dense embedding model's input sequence length
explanation: In multi-turn chat, messages like 'Tell me more about it' are ambiguous without context. Query rewriting uses prior turns to resolve what 'it' refers to, producing a self-contained query that retrieval systems can process without conversation history.

q: Enforcing a context token budget (e.g., 8K tokens for retrieved passages) in a RAG generation pipeline is important because:
options: The reranker's cross-encoder has a strict sequence-length limit and cannot process passages exceeding 512 tokens without truncation
Dense embedding models produce lower-quality vectors for passages longer than a few hundred tokens, requiring aggressive chunking
Including many passages increases generation temperature, causing the LLM to produce more hallucinated details in long-context scenarios
Irrelevant or redundant retrieved passages waste context tokens and can dilute truly relevant evidence, degrading answer accuracy
explanation: More context is not always better. Low-relevance passages introduce noise that distracts the generator and displace high-relevance ones. Careful context selection and budget management consistently improve LLM answer quality over indiscriminate document concatenation.

q: A bloom filter is used in a real-time personalization system to efficiently:
options: Check at retrieval time whether a candidate item was already shown to the user in the current session without materializing the full seen-item set
Project two session embedding vectors into a compact binary fingerprint space to approximate their cosine similarity quickly
Detect when the user's current session embedding has drifted significantly from their precomputed long-term profile embedding
Estimate the number of distinct items available in the ANN index without performing a full corpus scan before retrieval
explanation: Bloom filters support O(1) probabilistic set membership tests with very low memory overhead. They guarantee no false negatives (seen items are never re-surfaced) while accepting a small, tunable false positive rate for the already-seen check.

q: Demographic parity as a fairness constraint in a ranking system is often considered too rigid because:
options: Demographic parity applies only to binary classification outputs and cannot be computed or enforced on a ranked result list
Equalizing positive prediction rates across groups can require surfacing less relevant items for some groups, reducing overall quality
Demographic parity protects only legally enumerated attributes (race, gender) and ignores broader fairness dimensions of concern
Measuring demographic parity requires collecting sensitive demographic attributes, creating tension with data minimization obligations
explanation: If one group has fewer truly relevant items in the corpus, demographic parity forces the model to surface lower-relevance items to hit the equal-rate target. Equal opportunity — equating TPR — is usually a more practical and quality-preserving constraint.

q: Inverse propensity scoring and epsilon-greedy exploration both reduce selection bias in training data but differ in that:
options: IPS is applied at inference time to adjust the ranking scores of displayed items, while exploration determines which items are shown
IPS requires the model's predicted score for each candidate item, while exploration operates independently of any model prediction
IPS reweights existing historical logs post-hoc to correct for display policy bias, while exploration gathers new unbiased data by randomizing shown items
IPS is a training-time correction that reduces recommendation diversity, while exploration is an inference-time technique that increases it
explanation: IPS is a post-hoc statistical correction applied to logged data — it cannot recover information about items never shown. Exploration actively randomizes serving decisions to generate truly unbiased new training signal for under-explored items.

q: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling) is generally avoided for class-imbalance handling in high-dimensional embedding spaces because:
options: SMOTE doubles minority-class training time without modifying the loss function, requiring additional calibration to correct output probabilities
SMOTE ignores the temporal ordering of training examples, violating the recency assumption critical in time-series fraud detection
SMOTE creates synthetic examples indistinguishable from real positives, causing the model to memorize synthetic rather than true patterns
Interpolating between nearest neighbors in high-dimensional space produces vectors that fall in low-density regions unrepresentative of real data
explanation: In high dimensions, the curse of dimensionality makes nearest neighbors geometrically far apart. Interpolating between them creates embeddings that correspond to no plausible real sample, often degrading model quality relative to class-weight reweighting.

q: For fraud detection where chargeback labels arrive 30-90 days after transactions, the recommended strategy for the label-delay window is:
options: Apply semi-supervised learning on the unlabeled recent window using fraud probability signals, then fully retrain when confirmed labels arrive
Generate proxy labels using a high-precision rule-based engine during the label-delay period to maintain model recency without confirmed data
Freeze model updates until the full 90-day chargeback window closes, ensuring every retraining run uses only complete confirmed labels
Train only on the most recent 7-day window where most chargebacks are expected to arrive earliest according to issuer processing time
explanation: Semi-supervised learning leverages high-confidence fraud signals (extreme velocity, novel device) as pseudo-labels during the delay window. When confirmed chargebacks arrive, the model fully retrains, incorporating ground-truth labels.

q: In a production LLM conversational AI system, per-session multi-turn conversation state is most commonly stored in:
options: The LLM server's key-value (KV) cache, which persists transparently across turns for the duration of an inference process's uptime
A distributed low-latency store like Spanner with a per-session TTL, enabling rapid retrieval of context on each new conversation turn
A compressed session embedding in the user's long-term profile, generated offline by a summary encoder between sessions
Browser-side local storage or a session cookie, keeping conversation history on-device and eliminating server-side state management
explanation: Spanner provides globally consistent, low-latency reads needed to reconstruct conversation context within the LLM's response latency budget. KV caches are ephemeral and tied to a specific inference process; browser state is unreliable and device-specific.

q: The primary risk of warm-start retraining — initializing a new model from the previous model's weights — is:
options: Warm-start requires both the old and new checkpoints simultaneously in GPU memory, doubling peak memory usage during training
Model provenance tracking becomes complex since each warm-start model's behavior depends on the full training history of its predecessors
Biases or systematic errors in the prior model may be inherited and amplified if recent incoming data reinforces those same failure patterns
Starting from pre-trained weights changes the loss landscape topology, making standard learning rate schedules converge to poor local minima
explanation: Warm-start converges faster and requires less new data, but the new model is initialized from a potentially biased prior. If the old model underperformed for a specific user segment and new data does not counteract it, warm-start can preserve and amplify the disparity.

q: The three-tier fraud decision system (auto-approve / human review queue / auto-block) is designed to solve which specific operational problem?
options: Regulatory requirements mandating human sign-off on all transactions exceeding a specified dollar threshold in high-risk categories
Cold start challenges for new merchants lacking sufficient history to support a confident binary approve-or-block model decision
Real-time latency constraints that prevent the ML model from scoring all low-risk transactions within the 100ms serving SLA
Reviewer throughput limits — routing too many uncertain cases at low precision floods the queue and degrades review quality and speed
explanation: At 5% precision with 10,000 daily alerts, reviewers face 9,500 false alarms per day. Three-tier scoring restricts the human review queue to genuinely ambiguous cases within the ~200 cases/day reviewer capacity, maintaining precision and reviewer health.

q: In a search ranking system, a freshness classifier is introduced as a pre-retrieval step primarily to:
options: Detect time-sensitive queries such as breaking news events and apply a recency boost to recently indexed documents for those queries
Remove stale documents from the inverted index that have not been recrawled within the configured staleness expiration window
Route navigational versus informational queries to separate retrieval stacks that are each optimized for a distinct intent type
Predict how much user satisfaction would increase if current results were replaced with documents from the most recent 24-hour crawl
explanation: For evergreen queries, relevance dominates freshness. For time-sensitive queries (election results, stock prices), recency is paramount. The freshness classifier identifies which regime applies and activates a freshness boost selectively.

q: In a RAG system, retrieval quality gating prevents hallucination specifically by:
options: Launching a secondary higher-recall retrieval pass when the initial retrieval returns fewer than the minimum required number of passages
Returning 'I don't have enough information' when no retrieved passage exceeds a minimum cosine similarity threshold to the query
Filtering generated output tokens that do not appear verbatim in any retrieved passage before assembling the final response text
Routing each generated sentence through an NLI model to verify factual entailment before including it in the delivered response
explanation: When retrieval fails to find relevant context (low similarity scores), the LLM is likely to fabricate an answer from parametric memory. Gating forces the system to abstain rather than hallucinate, trading coverage for factual reliability.

q: In a multi-objective recommender, the weights in the scalarized score formula are best determined by:
options: Setting them proportional to the inverse variance of each signal, normalizing each objective's contribution to the combined final score
Using Pareto frontier analysis to identify efficient weight combinations and selecting the one that directly maximizes primary revenue
Running online A/B experiments that measure how different weight combinations affect the primary long-term metric like 7-day retention
Fitting a linear regression on historical session data to recover the implicit weights revealed by past user engagement behavior patterns
explanation: Multi-objective weights are tuned empirically through online experiments because the relationships between objectives and business outcomes are non-linear and context-dependent. Offline weight estimation consistently misses interaction effects that only appear in live traffic.

q: The practice of disaggregating model performance metrics by demographic slice rather than reporting only aggregate numbers is important because:
options: Per-slice metrics are statistically easier to interpret and avoid Bonferroni corrections that aggregate metrics often require
Regulatory compliance frameworks in most jurisdictions legally mandate per-demographic breakdowns in all submitted model documentation
Sliced metrics reduce A/B test decision variance by removing the confounding effect of mixing heterogeneous demographic subpopulations
An aggregate AUC of 0.94 can hide severe underperformance such as 0.72 AUC for specific groups representing a significant user base
explanation: Aggregate metrics mask disparate impacts. A model achieving 97% accuracy on the majority group and 72% on a 15% minority group still reports a high headline number. Sliced metrics force visibility of where the system fails specific communities.

q: Continuous online learning is distinguished from warm-start batch retraining in that continuous learning:
options: Applies incremental gradient updates on streaming data as it arrives, adapting the model to distribution changes within minutes
Initializes the next training run from the previous model's weights, training only on recent data to reduce compute and convergence time
Maintains a rolling data window and discards examples older than a cutoff, preventing the model from memorizing outdated patterns
Uses a learning rate schedule tied to the data arrival rate, automatically decelerating updates when the incoming stream slows down
explanation: Online learning applies gradient updates continuously as labeled data arrives — the model is never fully retrained from scratch. Warm-start retraining still performs discrete batch training jobs; it just initializes from prior weights to accelerate convergence.

q: "User-category affinity" — how often a user clicks content in a specific category — is classified as a cross feature rather than a user or item feature because:
options: It is computed via a SQL cross-join of the user table and item category table during feature store materialization
It captures an interaction between the user dimension and the item dimension that neither user profile alone nor category alone encodes
It spans multiple user sessions and multiple item types simultaneously, making it a cross-session and cross-item derived aggregate
It must be recomputed whenever the user's session crosses a category boundary, qualifying it as a session-level derived signal
explanation: Knowing user A has a high overall click rate and item B is in Sports does not reveal whether user A likes Sports. The cross feature user_A × Sports directly encodes this joint preference, which is far more predictive than either dimension independently.

q: A GRU or lightweight transformer is used to encode the current session's action sequence in real-time personalization because:
options: GRUs have fewer trainable parameters than an equivalent MLP, reducing inference latency when the session encoder runs on CPU hardware
Session sequences consist of discrete item ID tokens, and GRUs are architecturally specialized for tokenized inputs over dense features
The session model must capture temporal ordering so that recent actions — like three consecutive cooking videos — immediately shift the embedding
GRUs impose internal sparsity constraints on hidden states, preventing the session embedding from converging to a constant across interactions
explanation: Order matters for intent: watching cooking content in the past 10 minutes signals cooking interest, not just any interest. Recurrent and attention architectures explicitly model sequential structure; a bag-of-items aggregation would miss this temporal signal.

q: Federated learning combined with differential privacy is most valuable for ML personalization specifically when:
options: Training a global ranking model on server-side impression logs where feature statistics vary considerably across geographic regions
Distilling a large server-side foundation model into a compact version that can meet a recommendation system's strict latency budget
Computing user embeddings from streaming click data arriving at high volume from a small number of known and trusted data centers
Training personalization models on sensitive on-device data such as local search or health history without transmitting raw records centrally
explanation: Federated learning trains on-device without raw data leaving the device. Differential privacy adds calibrated noise to gradient updates, preventing reconstruction of individual users' data even from aggregated model updates — enabling privacy-preserving personalization.

q: Approximate nearest-neighbor algorithms like ScaNN can search 1 billion embeddings in ~4ms versus ~5,000ms for brute-force search primarily because:
options: They avoid computing distances to the vast majority of vectors by partitioning or quantizing the space, accepting a small recall loss for massive speedup
They exploit hardware-specific SIMD vector instructions unavailable to the general-purpose matrix libraries used in brute-force distance computation
They operate in INT8 reduced-precision arithmetic, inheriting all quantization speedup benefits as a direct side effect of index construction
They parallelize distance computation across GPU tensor cores, whereas brute-force nearest-neighbor search is constrained to sequential processing
explanation: ANN methods limit the search to a fraction of the index using tree partitions, quantization, or graph shortcuts. ScaNN combines partitioning and anisotropic quantization to skip ~99.9% of distance computations, achieving the ~1000x speedup over exact search.

q: Continuous online learning is preferred over weekly batch retraining for an ads click prediction model specifically because:
options: Online learning requires fewer labeled examples per weight update, lowering label collection cost when click labels are expensive
Advertiser inventory, user behavior, and seasonal trends shift rapidly; online learning adapts within hours rather than waiting days for a batch cycle
Batch retraining accumulates updates over the entire historical dataset, causing the model to overfit older patterns at the expense of recent trends
Online learning eliminates the need for periodic A/B tests by continuously evaluating changes through an implicit multi-armed bandit on live traffic
explanation: Ad models trained on last week's data can already be stale: new advertisers appear, user interests shift, and holiday patterns emerge. Continuous training on the last 24-48 hours of click data keeps the model aligned with today's distribution.

q: A session-aware ranking model using a transformer over session events has a key advantage over a model using only a daily-recomputed user embedding in that it:
options: Eliminates the need for a feature store entirely by computing all required user features, item features, and session context from raw event logs inline at prediction time
Can be trained end-to-end jointly with the candidate retrieval model, reducing the count of separately deployed model services
Reflects within-session intent shifts immediately — three consecutive cooking videos immediately boost cooking-content rankings without waiting for daily recomputation
Reduces cold start severity since session signals exist from the first interaction, whereas long-term embeddings require substantial accumulated history
explanation: Long-term embeddings encode historical preferences but cannot react to real-time intent. The session model re-encodes after each action, so a user who pivots to cooking content mid-session sees immediately updated recommendations.

q: A model card in Google's Responsible AI framework primarily documents:
options: The model's formal architecture specification, training loss function, and hyperparameter configuration for internal engineering reproduction
The legal usage terms governing permitted and prohibited applications of the model across different product and research contexts
Training infrastructure details including TPU-hours consumed and estimated carbon footprint for corporate sustainability reporting
The model's intended use cases, training data characteristics, performance across demographic groups, and known limitations for transparency
explanation: Model cards are transparency artifacts for external stakeholders and regulators. They describe what the model does, what data trained it, how it performs across different groups, and what failure modes exist — not internal implementation details or legal terms.
